diagrams.title: Class as Blueprint, Objects as Instances
diagrams.description: A visual representation showing the relationship between a class (the blueprint/template) and objects (concrete instances created from that blueprint). The class defines the structure, and objects are actual runtime entities with their own memory.
elements: A large class box labeled 'Car Class' with sections for attributes (color, model, speed) and methods (accelerate, brake)
Two smaller object boxes connected to the class with dashed lines, labeled 'Car object1' and 'Car object2'
Each object box shows actual values (e.g., 'color: red', 'speed: 60' for object1 and 'color: blue', 'speed: 0' for object2)
Arrow labels showing 'instantiates' or 'is instance of' relationship
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand the fundamental difference between a class (abstract template) and objects (concrete instances), which is crucial for OOP understanding.

diagrams.title: C++ Access Modifiers: Public, Private, Protected
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the three access modifiers in C++ with clear visualization of what each modifier controls and who can access what. Uses a class diagram with three distinct colored sections representing each access level.
elements: A class rectangle divided into three horizontal sections with different colors (green=public, yellow=protected, red=private)
Public section with '+' symbol containing methods like startEngine() and getSpeed()
Protected section with '#' symbol containing attributes like engineStatus
Private section with '-' symbol containing attributes like serialNumber and fuelLevel
Three external entities: 'Derived Class', 'Friend Functions', and 'Other Classes' with arrows showing access permissions
diagrams.purpose: Explains the three C++ access modifiers (public, private, protected) and their visibility rules, which is essential for encapsulation and data hiding in C++.

diagrams.title: Complete Class Definition with Modifiers
diagrams.description: A comprehensive example showing a BankAccount class with all three access modifiers applied to different members, demonstrating proper encapsulation practices in C++.
elements: A class box labeled 'BankAccount' with the class name at top
Private section (red) containing 'accountNumber (string)', 'balance (double)', 'pin (int)' - data members hidden from outside
Protected section (yellow) containing 'transactionHistory (vector)' - accessible by derived classes
Constructor and destructor shown in public section
Visual indicators showing encapsulation boundary (the class wall)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates a complete, well-encapsulated C++ class showing how access modifiers are used in practice to hide data while providing a public interface.
